perturbation.original_text: Bioamber and Cargill agree to waive any and all claims against each other for consequential, punitive, incidental, special, or other forms of "exemplary" losses whether arising in contract, warranty, tort (including negligence), strict liability, or otherwise, including any losses relating to lost use, lost profits, lost business, damage to reputation, or lost or diminished financing unless such claims are based on a Party's gross negligence or willful misconduct.
perturbation.changed_text: Bioamber and Cargill agree to waive any and all claims against each other for consequential, punitive, incidental, special, or other forms of "exemplary" losses whether arising in contract, warranty, tort (including negligence), strict liability, or otherwise, including any losses relating to lost use, lost profits, lost business, damage to reputation, or lost or diminished financing.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exception for 'gross negligence or willful misconduct,' the clause now attempts to waive claims even for intentional or grossly negligent actions. This violates the principle that parties cannot contract away liability for their own gross negligence or intentional misconduct, which is against public policy and state laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statute § 604.10 addresses the enforceability of agreements related to liability for negligence, particularly in construction contracts. While this specific statute addresses construction, it reflects a broader principle under Minnesota law and public policy that attempts to waive liability for gross negligence or intentional misconduct are generally disfavored and may be deemed unenforceable. The changed text attempts to waive all claims, effectively including those arising from gross negligence or willful misconduct, contradicting this principle.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 604.10 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes CIVIL ACTIONS Chapter 604 Section 604.10 604.09 604.101 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 604 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 604.10 Version List Recent History 1998 604.10 Amended 1998 c 2 s 1 604.10 ECONOMIC LOSS ARISING FROM THE SALE OF GOODS. (a) Economic loss that arises from a sale of goods that is due to damage to tangible property other than the goods sold may be recovered in tort as well as in contract, but economic loss that arises from a sale of goods between parties who are each merchants in goods of the kind is not recoverable in tort. (b) Economic loss that arises from a sale of goods, between merchants, that is not due to damage to tangible property other than the goods sold may not be recovered in tort. (c) The economic loss recoverable in tort under this section does not include economic loss due to damage to the goods themselves. (d) The economic loss recoverable in tort under this section does not include economic loss incurred by a manufacturer of goods arising from damage to the manufactured goods and caused by a component of the goods. (e) This section shall not be interpreted to bar tort causes of action based upon fraud or fraudulent or intentional misrepresentation or limit remedies for those actions. History: 1991 c 352 s 2 ; 1993 c 91 s 1 ; 1Sp1998 c 2 s 1 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The original text includes an exception for claims based on gross negligence or willful misconduct, while the changed text removes this exception, attempting to waive all claims, even those arising from such actions. The Law Explanation states that Minnesota law generally disfavors attempts to waive liability for gross negligence or intentional misconduct and they may be deemed unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Minnesota Statute § 604.10, addresses the enforceability of agreements related to liability, and while it primarily concerns economic loss arising from the sale of goods, subsection (e) specifies that the section should not bar tort causes of action based upon fraud or fraudulent or intentional misrepresentation or limit remedies for those actions, reinforcing the principle against waiving liability for intentional misconduct. By removing the exception, the changed text contradicts this principle, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Development Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Minnesota, United States of America, disregarding its conflicts of law rules.
perturbation.changed_text: This Development Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of contract as decided by Cargill, without regard to any jurisdiction's laws or rules.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text removes the reference to Minnesota law and allows Cargill to decide which laws apply, which contradicts standard legal practices. It introduces ambiguity and may attempt to override consumer protection laws or mandatory regulations that would otherwise apply under Minnesota law, and also introduces a clear bias.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statute § 336.1-301 outlines the principles of freedom of contract but acknowledges limitations imposed by mandatory rules of law. The modified text, which allows Cargill to arbitrarily decide the applicable contract laws, violates this statute because it attempts to bypass mandatory legal provisions in place to protect parties to the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 336.1-301 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 336 Section 336.1-301 336.1-209 336.1-302 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 336 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 336.1-301 Version List Recent History 2024 336.1-301 Amended 2024 c 93 art 1 s 3 2004 336.1-301 New 2004 c 162 art 1 s 16 336.1-301 TERRITORIAL APPLICABILITY; PARTIES' POWER TO CHOOSE APPLICABLE LAW. (a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), the Uniform Commercial Code applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) section 336.2-402 ; (2) sections 336.2A-105 and 336.2A-106 ; (3) section 336.4-102 ; (4) section 336.4A-507 ; (5) section 336.5-116 ; (6) section 336.8-110 ; (7) sections 336.9-301 through 336.9-307 ; (8) section 336.12-107 . History: 2004 c 162 art 1 s 16 ; 2024 c 93 art 1 s 3 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Minnesota Statute § 336.1-301. The original text specifies Minnesota law, but the changed text allows Cargill to choose the applicable laws, potentially bypassing mandatory legal provisions designed to protect parties, as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, subsection (a) of § 336.1-301, states that parties may agree on which state or nation's law will govern their rights and duties when a transaction has a reasonable relation to both that state/nation and Minnesota. The modified text, by allowing Cargill sole discretion, could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent this principle, particularly if it chooses laws that offer less protection than Minnesota law would. This is an Omission Legal Contradiction, since it removes the legally standard jurisdiction, opening the contract to less consumer protection.

perturbation.original_text: Cargill shall have no liability to Bioamber for Bioamber's acts or failure to act with respect to such Third Party Patents.
perturbation.changed_text: Cargill shall have no liability to Bioamber for any failure to act with respect to such Third Party Patents.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'Bioamber's acts or' means that even if Cargill's INACTION regarding known risks from Third Party Patents directly harms Bioamber, Cargill disclaims all liability. This conflicts with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in contracts under Minnesota law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although not a Minnesota statute, the principle of good faith and fair dealing is a cornerstone of contract law recognized by Minnesota courts, often referring to the Restatement (Second) of Contracts. Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 205 states that 'Every contract imposes upon each party a duty of good faith and fair dealing in its performance and its enforcement.' By disclaiming all liability for inaction, even when that inaction foreseeably harms Bioamber regarding third-party patents related to the Work Plan (Section 13.1), the modified text violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes 'Bioamber's acts or' from the original contract, meaning Cargill now disclaims liability even for their INACTION, potentially harming Bioamber regarding Third Party Patents. This contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines good faith as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'faithful performance of duties,' and states that a 'breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability.' By removing liability for inaction, the modified text permits Cargill to act without good faith, contradicting the principle in the scraped snippet.
